narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 57.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which was the key reason your application was rejected—they use this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed. Your profile includes being in the bottom decile for Year 3 grades at your school, second-bottom for Year 1, an LSAT score of 35, an undergraduate GPA of 3.20, upper-middle family income, identifying as female and White, and planning full-time enrollment. Compared to typical students, your Year 1 and especially Year 3 deciles are much lower than the average around the fifth or sixth decile, your LSAT is a bit below the usual 36.7, while your GPA matches the norm and your family income is solidly above average. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 school decile, which had the biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward bar failure. Next in importance was your LSAT score, also nudging the odds toward failure compared to stronger scorers. Your Year 1 decile ranked third, similarly contributing to the risk assessment. Family income came fourth, with your upper-middle status still tilting slightly against you in the model's view, followed by your undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time plans, all playing smaller roles in the same direction. Overall, these factors together painted a picture riskier than the typical applicant, who tends to have mid-range school deciles, higher LSATs, and similar demographics. While your strengths like a solid GPA and family support helped a little, the lower early performance indicators outweighed them. To boost your future bar exam chances, focus on what you can realistically improve: consider retaking the LSAT to aim above 37, as higher scores strongly correlate with passing, and build stronger study habits early in law school to climb those deciles next time. Seeking targeted tutoring or bar prep courses could also address patterns from your undergrad and prior grades. With dedication, you can shift this prediction in your favor for the next application cycle. Keep pushing—you've got a good foundation to build on.
